questions.question: Explain CPU Utilization and Throughput as scheduling criteria. How are they related?
keyPoints: CPU Utilization is the percentage of time the CPU is busy executing processes
Throughput is the number of processes completed per unit time
They are positively related - higher CPU utilization typically leads to higher throughput
Higher throughput also contributes to better CPU utilization as more work is completed

questions.question: Define Turnaround Time and Waiting Time. How is Turnaround Time calculated?
keyPoints: Turnaround Time = Time from process arrival to completion
Waiting Time = Total time process spends in ready queue
Turnaround Time = Waiting Time + Burst Time (execution time)
These metrics evaluate different aspects of process performance

questions.question: Why is Response Time critical for interactive systems? Give an example.
keyPoints: Response Time measures time from request to first output
Interactive systems require quick feedback for user satisfaction
Examples: typing in editor, clicking menu, web browsing
Too long response time makes system feel unresponsive

questions.question: Explain the concept of Fairness in CPU scheduling. Why is it important?
keyPoints: Fairness ensures all processes receive reasonable CPU time
Prevents starvation where some processes never get CPU access
Fair scheduling gives equal opportunity to all processes
Without fairness, some processes may be indefinitely delayed

questions.question: Differentiate between Batch, Interactive, and Real-time systems based on their primary scheduling criteria.
keyPoints: Batch systems prioritize Throughput and CPU Utilization
Interactive systems prioritize Response Time
Real-time systems prioritize Meeting Deadlines
Each system type has different performance requirements

questions.question: What is Balancing as a scheduling criterion? How does it improve system performance?
keyPoints: Balancing ensures all system resources (CPU, I/O) are utilized
Prevents CPU from being idle while I/O devices are busy
Prevents I/O from being idle while CPU is busy
Results in overall improved system efficiency

questions.question: A process arrives at time 0 with burst time 10 units. It waits for 5 units before execution starts. Calculate its Waiting Time and Turnaround Time.
keyPoints: Waiting Time = Time spent in ready queue = 5 units
Burst Time = 10 units
Turnaround Time = Waiting Time + Burst Time = 5 + 10 = 15 units
Verification: If it completes at time 15, Turnaround = 15 - 0 = 15

questions.question: Describe five main scheduling criteria used to evaluate CPU scheduling algorithms. For each criterion, explain its significance and identify which type of system (batch, interactive, real-time) would prioritize it.
keyPoints: CPU Utilization: Keep CPU busy - important for all systems, especially batch
Throughput: Processes completed per unit - critical for batch systems
Turnaround Time: Total time from arrival to completion - important for batch
Waiting Time: Time in ready queue - affects overall system efficiency
Response Time: Time to first response - critical for interactive systems
Fairness: Equal CPU distribution - prevents starvation in all systems
Meeting Deadlines: Critical for real-time systems
Real-time also needs predictability and guaranteed completion before deadlines

questions.question: Consider three processes P1, P2, and P3 with burst times 15, 5, and 8 units respectively. They all arrive at time 0. Calculate: (a) Average Waiting Time (b) Average Turnaround Time (c) Throughput if all complete in 28 time units.
keyPoints: Using FCFS (P1, P2, P3 order): P1 waits 0, P2 waits 15, P3 waits 20
Total Waiting Time = 0 + 15 + 20 = 35, Average = 35/3 = 11.67 units
Turnaround: P1 = 15, P2 = 20, P3 = 28, Average = (15+20+28)/3 = 21 units
Throughput = 3 processes / 28 time units = 0.107 processes per unit time
For SJF (optimal): Order P2, P3, P1 - different values result

questions.question: Explain the trade-offs between different scheduling criteria. Can all criteria be optimized simultaneously? Justify your answer with examples.
keyPoints: Not all criteria can be optimized simultaneously - trade-offs exist
SJF minimizes waiting time but may cause starvation
FCFS maximizes throughput but increases waiting time
Round Robin provides fairness but may increase turnaround time
Response time vs throughput trade-off is common
Example: Short processes complete faster (good waiting) but longer processes wait more
In practice, systems prioritize based on their purpose (batch vs interactive vs real-time)

questions.question: Discuss how scheduling criteria affect the design and selection of scheduling algorithms in operating systems. Provide examples of algorithms optimized for specific criteria.
keyPoints: Algorithm selection depends on system type and primary goals
SJF optimizes for waiting time but is impractical (requires burst time prediction)
FCFS is simple but poor for waiting time - used as baseline
Round Robin optimizes for response time and fairness - used in time-sharing
Priority scheduling can address fairness but may cause starvation
Real-time systems need deadline-driven algorithms like EDF
Throughput optimization often conflicts with response time requirements
